revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石動権現]]


'''{{ill|Isurugi Gongen|ja|石動権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Gongen|qq=}}''' (いするぎごんげん) is a deity of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} that emerged from the fusion of the {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain Worship|1=Mountain worship}} and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} traditions of {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|qid=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} and {{ill|Mount Sekidō|en|Mount Sekidō|qid=Q11585200|lt=Isurugi Mountain|1=Mount Sekidō}}. It enshrines {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}} as its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha|1=Honji suijaku}}, and before the implementation of {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|qid=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}, it was venerated at the various '''Isurugi Shrines''' after being invited from {{ill|Isurugi Mountain Tenpyō Temple|ja|石動山天平寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Mountain Tenpyō Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Overview ==
According to the ''Isurugi Mountain Ancient Origins'' (『石動山古縁起』), in the 6th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, the hermit {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|方道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} opened the mountain, and in the first year of the {{ill|Yōrō era|ja|養老元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|717|en|717|qid=Q23532|lt=717|1=717}}), the monk {{ill|Chitoku Shōnin|ja|智徳上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chitoku Shōnin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ascended it and, in the 8th year of {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|en|Tenpyō-shōhō|qid=Q1373169|lt=Tenpyō Shōhō|1=Tenpyō-shōhō}} ({{ill|756|en|756|qid=Q31121|lt=756|1=756}}), constructed the Great Ceremonial Hall (lecture hall). On the other hand, a founding tradition attributed to {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|qid=Q2388439|lt=Taichō|1=Taichō}} has long existed, and the ''Shin Enki'' (『新縁起』) authored by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|en|Hayashi Razan|qid=Q558279|lt=Hayashi Razan|1=Hayashi Razan}} in the early modern period also asserts that it was established by Taichō.

In the Middle Ages, ''Isurugi Shugyō'' reached its zenith, with {{ill|Isurugi Mountain Tenpyō Temple|ja|石動山天平寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Mountain Tenpyō Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} counting over 360 establishments and 3,000 followers.

{| class="wikitable"
! !!Former Name!!Honji
|-
|rowspan=5|{{ill|Five Gongen|ja|五所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579263|1=Q135579263}}
|{{ill|Ōmiya|en|Ōmiya|qid=Q1902493|lt=Ōmiya|1=Ōmiya}} || {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}}
|-
|{{ill|Kakujin|ja|客人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakujin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}}
|-
|{{ill|Himiya|ja|火宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Sacred Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|2=ja|3=聖観音|lt=Shōkannon|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred Kannon|qq=}}
|-
|{{ill|Umiya|ja|梅宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Victory Jizō|1=Kṣitigarbha}}
|-
|{{ill|Kenmiya|ja|剣宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|qid=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}
|}

== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism and Haibutsu Kishaku ==
Due to the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=Shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1970535}} issued during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the Shugendō-based {{ill|Isurugi Gongen|ja|石動権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Gongen|qq=}} was abolished. In the 3rd year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|1870|en|1870|qid=Q7741|lt=1870|1=1870}}), the Shingon temple {{ill|Isurugi Mountain Tenpyō Temple|ja|石動山天平寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Mountain Tenpyō Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was forced into abeyance and reorganized as the {{ill|Isurugi Shrine|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Shrine|1=Isurugi Hikojinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11381009}} ({{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}). Many of the Isurugi shrines nationwide have since become Shinto shrines or Five-Shrine complexes enshrining {{ill|Isurugi Hiko|ja|伊須流岐比古神|lt=Isurugi Hiko|lt_ja=石動彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579264|1=Isurugi Hiko}} and others as their deities.
